text: The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis to carefully liberate the loop of small bowel from adhesions, ensuring no serosal damage occurs during the process.
The surgeon carefully dissects a portion of the rectus sheath adjacent to the adherent loop of bowel, aiming to liberate the bowel with slightly free margins to prevent serosal damage.
The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to separate adhesions between loops of the small bowel, ensuring gentle manipulation to avoid serosal damage.
The surgeon uses a cold knife to carefully separate adhesions between the loops of the bowel, where the separation is minimal, ensuring gentle dissection to avoid serosal damage.
The surgeon carefully liberates the bowel from adhesions using a cold knife, ensuring minimal traction to separate the tissue and proceed with the surgical steps.
The surgeon uses scissors to perform both blunt and sharp dissection, carefully separating the adherent bowel from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to apply traction while performing cold dissection with scissors. The bowel, infiltrated inside the rectus sheath due to a previous burst abdomen, is being carefully separated.
The surgeon carefully separates the loop of the intestine from surrounding adhesions, ensuring the dissection is gentle to avoid serosal damage.
The surgeon prepares to suture the serosal layer of the small bowel using 3-0 silk in response to any inadvertent serosal damage that may occur during the procedure.

The surgeon creates windows through the loops of bowel using laparoscopic instruments, facilitating the separation of bowel loops for easier dissection. A 30-degree camera system is utilized to navigate these spaces at optimal angles.
The surgeon releases adhesions using a cold knife, taking advantage of the avascular plane for faster dissection. The laparoscopic shearer is used primarily for traction during this process.

The surgeon identifies a loop of bowel rotated along the mesentery and adherent to the anterior abdominal wall. The mesentery serves as a visual guide to facilitate dissection in the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer for traction and a cold knife for dissection, demonstrating gentle hand movements. The port positioning is carefully adjusted to achieve correct triangulation, minimizing strain on the shoulder.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect the loops of bowel, ensuring the plane of dissection remains correct to maintain a bloodless field, even when entering fibrotic planes. A portion of the rectus sheath is left attached to the bowel loops to preserve the serosa's integrity.
A 0.5 cm margin of the rectus sheath is dissected away from the anterior abdominal wall, preparing it for further liberation.

The camera is repositioned to face the anterior abdominal wall, where the surgeon uses a shearer to perform blunt dissection, separating the bowel from the anterior abdominal wall. A large adherent section of bowel is visible, which is being gently liberated from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon carefully navigates below the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the bladder which is visibly adherent to the bowel. The bladder is pulled up, requiring cautious technique to avoid damage during dissection.
The surgeon carefully cuts closer to the bowel, preserving the surrounding fat tissue, which is visually identifiable as belonging either to the bladder or the large intestine.

The surgeon observes a firm adhesion of the loop of ileum to the anterior abdominal wall. The camera is tilted for optimal visualization. The laparoscopic shearer is used to dissect the adhesion, preserving the serosal aspect of the ileum while sacrificing part of the rectus sheath. Due to severe fibrosis, the shearer is preferred over a cold knife. Peristalsis testing is performed by blocking the bowel proximally and distally to check for potential injury.

An artificial dye test is performed to identify any potential rent in the tissue. The loop of bowel is visible slightly above the sheath, and the surgeon uses the shearer to separate it from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon liberates a section of the anterior abdominal wall from the loop of ileum. The uterus remains obscured, but a portion of the ovary is visible along the right lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon performs further dissection to separate the right colon, specifically the cecum, from adhesions in the surgical plane. The appendix, although not yet visible, is noted as an important anatomical landmark during this process.

The bladder is visibly adherent to the bowel, completely covering the uterus. The bladder's peritoneum is absent, likely due to previous surgical maneuvers, and it is firmly attached to the bowel along the dome.

The surgeon performs further dissection to separate the colon from the loops of ileum, while the uterine fundus is likely visible below, with the ovary being used as a reference point during the procedure.
The surgeon uses blunt traction to separate the identified plane, focusing on separating the colon. The ileocecal junction and appendix are important landmarks in this dissection process.

The surgeon continues dissection, revealing the under aspect of the sigmoid mesentery with visible adhesions. The appendix is identified as it enters the pelvis, appearing to be adhered within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Bissinger bipolar device to apply gentle traction while performing sharp dissection with a shearer. The appendix is visualized entering the pelvis, and the ileocecal junction is identified as adherent to the ovary.
